a:
Spring is a comprehensive, lightweight, open-source framework for building Java applications.
It provides infrastructure support for developing Java applications, including dependency injection, aspect-oriented programming, and transaction management.
Spring promotes loose coupling through dependency injection and inversion of control (IoC).
It offers modules for data access, web applications, security, testing, and more.
Spring can be used for both standalone and enterprise-level applications.
It integrates easily with other frameworks and technologies, such as Hibernate, JPA, and JMS.

Dependency Injection is a design pattern that allows objects to receive their dependencies from an external source rather than creating them internally.
In Spring, DI is achieved through constructor injection, setter injection, or field injection.
It promotes loose coupling and easier unit testing by decoupling object creation from business logic.
Spring's IoC container manages the lifecycle and dependencies of beans automatically.
DI helps in managing complex application dependencies and configuration in a centralized way.
It improves code maintainability and flexibility by allowing easy swapping of implementations.

IoC is a principle where the control of object creation and management is transferred from the application code to a container or framework.
In Spring, the IoC container is responsible for instantiating, configuring, and assembling beans.
IoC enables loose coupling between components by removing direct dependencies.
It allows for better separation of concerns and easier testing.
IoC is implemented in Spring through dependency injection and bean configuration.
It supports modular and scalable application design.

q: What are the different types of Spring Bean scopes?
a:
Singleton: Only one instance of the bean is created per Spring container (default scope).
Prototype: A new instance is created every time the bean is requested from the container.
Request: A new bean instance is created for each HTTP request (web applications only).
Session: A new bean instance is created for each HTTP session (web applications only).
Application: A single bean instance is created for the lifecycle of a ServletContext (web applications only).
WebSocket: A single bean instance is created for the lifecycle of a WebSocket session (web applications only).

q: What is the use of @Autowired annotation?
a:
@Autowired is used for automatic dependency injection in Spring.
It can be applied to constructors, fields, or setter methods to inject collaborating beans.
Spring resolves and injects the required bean automatically based on type (and optionally by name with @Qualifier).
It reduces boilerplate code and manual wiring of dependencies.
It supports both required and optional dependencies (using required=false).
It works with both XML and annotation-based configuration.

q: What is the difference between @Component, @Service, @Repository, and @Controller?

a:
Spring Boot is a framework built on top of Spring to simplify application setup and development.
It provides auto-configuration, embedded servers (Tomcat, Jetty), and production-ready features.
Spring Boot eliminates the need for extensive XML configuration and boilerplate code.
It offers starter dependencies for rapid development and easy integration with other technologies.
Spring Boot includes built-in tools for monitoring, health checks, and metrics.
It is ideal for creating stand-alone, production-grade Spring applications quickly.

q: What is the purpose of application.properties or application.yml?
a:
These files are used to configure application settings in a Spring Boot application.
They allow you to set database URLs, server ports, logging levels, and other properties.
They support hierarchical and environment-specific configuration (e.g., application-dev.properties).
They can be used to externalize configuration for different deployment environments.
Both .properties and .yml formats are supported for flexibility.
They help keep configuration separate from code, improving maintainability.

q: How do you handle exceptions in Spring Boot?
a:
Use @ControllerAdvice to define global exception handlers for controllers.
Use @ExceptionHandler methods to handle specific exceptions.
Return custom error responses or views for different exception types.
Spring Boot provides a default error page and JSON error response for REST APIs.
You can customize error handling using ErrorController or ResponseEntityExceptionHandler.
Exception handling improves user experience and application robustness.

Spring Data JPA is a part of the Spring Data project for simplifying data access using JPA (Java Persistence API).
It provides repository interfaces like JpaRepository and CrudRepository for CRUD operations.
It supports custom query methods using method naming conventions and @Query annotation.
Spring Data JPA handles entity mapping, transactions, and pagination automatically.
It integrates with various databases and supports auditing and projections.
It reduces boilerplate code for data access layers.

q: What is the difference between Monolithic and Microservice Architecture?
a:
Monolithic Architecture | Microservice Architecture
Single, unified application with tightly coupled components. | Composed of independent, loosely coupled services for each business feature.
All modules are deployed together as a single unit. | Each service can be developed, deployed, and scaled independently.
Technology stack is usually uniform across the application. | Different services can use different technology stacks and databases.
Scaling requires scaling the entire application. | Only the required services can be scaled as needed.
Failure in one module can affect the entire application. | Failure in one service does not impact others directly.
Simple to develop initially but becomes complex as it grows. | More complex to set up but easier to maintain and scale in the long run.

q: How does Spring Boot interact with the databases?
a:
Spring Boot uses Spring Data JPA, JDBC, or other data access modules to interact with databases.
Entities are defined using @Entity annotation, mapping Java classes to database tables.
Repositories like JpaRepository and CrudRepository provide CRUD operations without boilerplate code.
Database connection details (URL, username, password, dialect) are configured in application.properties or application.yml.
Supports automatic schema generation and database initialization using properties like spring.jpa.hibernate.ddl-auto.
Enables transaction management, connection pooling, and supports multiple databases (MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, etc.).

MVC is a design pattern that separates an application into three main components: Model, View, and Controller.
Model: Represents the application's data and business logic.
View: Handles the display and presentation of data to the user (UI).
Controller: Processes user input, interacts with the model, and selects the view for response.
Promotes separation of concerns, making code more modular and maintainable.
Spring MVC implements this pattern for building robust web applications.

Occurs when a lazily loaded association or collection is accessed outside the Hibernate session.
Common in ORM frameworks like Hibernate and JPA when using FetchType.LAZY.
Can be fixed by fetching the data within an open session or using FetchType.EAGER for immediate loading.
DTO projection or @Transactional methods can help avoid this exception.
Indicates improper session management or detached entities.
Best practice is to design data access layers to avoid lazy loading issues.

Metaspace is a memory area in Java 8 and above that stores class metadata (class definitions, methods, etc.).
Replaces the PermGen space used in earlier Java versions.
Metaspace grows automatically as needed, limited only by system memory.
Helps avoid OutOfMemoryError: PermGen space issues from previous Java versions.
Can be tuned using JVM options like -XX:MaxMetaspaceSize.
Improves class loading and unloading efficiency in large applications.

@Transactional is an annotation used to define transactional boundaries for methods or classes.
Ensures that a series of operations are executed within a single transaction context.
If an exception occurs, the transaction is rolled back automatically.
Supports configuration of isolation levels, propagation behavior, and rollback rules.
Can be applied at the service or repository layer for data consistency.
Improves reliability and integrity of database operations.

q: What is Aspect in Spring Boot?
a:
Aspect-Oriented Programming (AOP) allows separation of cross-cutting concerns like logging, security, and transactions.
Aspects are modular units of code that can be applied across different parts of an application.
In Spring, aspects are defined using @Aspect annotation and advice types like @Before, @After, and @Around.
Helps keep business logic clean and focused by moving repetitive code to aspects.
Supports pointcuts to specify where advice should be applied.
Improves maintainability and reduces code duplication.

Spring Boot provides scheduling support using the @Scheduled annotation.
Tasks can be scheduled to run at fixed intervals, delays, or cron expressions.
Enable scheduling by adding @EnableScheduling to a configuration class.
Common use cases include periodic data cleanup, report generation, and notifications.
Supports both simple and complex scheduling needs with cron syntax.
Scheduled methods must be void and have no arguments.

Spring Security is a powerful framework for authentication and authorization in Java applications.
Supports a wide range of security features, including form login, OAuth2, JWT, and LDAP integration.
Provides method-level security using annotations like @PreAuthorize and @Secured.
Enables protection against common vulnerabilities such as CSRF, XSS, and session fixation.
Highly customizable to fit different security requirements.
Integrates seamlessly with Spring Boot and Spring MVC.

Dependency Injection (DI) is a design pattern that allows objects to receive their dependencies from an external source.
Spring supports DI through constructor, setter, and field injection.
Promotes loose coupling and easier testing by decoupling object creation from business logic.
Spring's IoC container manages the lifecycle and dependencies of beans automatically.
DI helps in managing complex application dependencies and configuration in a centralized way.
Improves code maintainability and flexibility by allowing easy swapping of implementations.

A Spring Bean is an object managed by the Spring IoC container.
Beans are defined using annotations like @Component, @Service, @Repository, or @Bean in configuration.
Beans can have different scopes (singleton, prototype, request, session, etc.).
Spring manages the lifecycle, dependencies, and configuration of beans.
Beans can be injected into other beans using dependency injection.
Central to building modular and testable Spring applications.

q: What is the Spring Boot flow?
a:
Application starts with a class annotated with @SpringBootApplication.
Spring Boot auto-configures beans and application settings based on dependencies.
Dependency injection wires up controllers, services, and repositories.
REST controllers handle HTTP requests and responses.
Service layer contains business logic, and repository layer handles data access.
Application connects to the database and exposes endpoints for clients.

Inversion of Control (IoC) is a design principle where the control of object creation and management is transferred to a container or framework.
In Spring, the IoC container is responsible for instantiating, configuring, and assembling beans.
IoC enables loose coupling between components by removing direct dependencies.
It allows for better separation of concerns and easier testing.
IoC is implemented in Spring through dependency injection and bean configuration.
It supports modular and scalable application design.

@Entity is a JPA annotation used to mark a class as a persistent entity.
Each entity maps to a table in the database, and fields map to columns.
Requires a primary key field annotated with @Id.
Entities can have relationships with other entities (OneToOne, OneToMany, etc.).
Used in ORM frameworks like Hibernate and Spring Data JPA.
Supports automatic schema generation and mapping.

q: What are the types of Spring Beans?
a:
Singleton: Only one instance of the bean is created per Spring container (default scope).
Prototype: A new instance is created every time the bean is requested from the container.
Request: A new bean instance is created for each HTTP request (web applications only).
Session: A new bean instance is created for each HTTP session (web applications only).
Application: A single bean instance is created for the lifecycle of a ServletContext (web applications only).
WebSocket: A single bean instance is created for the lifecycle of a WebSocket session (web applications only).

Singleton: Default scope, creates a single shared instance per Spring container.
Prototype: Creates a new instance every time it is requested.
Request: Scoped to an HTTP request (valid in web-aware applications).
Session: Scoped to an HTTP session (valid in web-aware applications).
Application: Scoped to the lifecycle of a ServletContext (valid in web-aware applications).
WebSocket: Scoped to a WebSocket session (valid in web-aware applications).

q: What are the main modules of Spring?
a:
Core Container: Includes Core, Beans, Context, and Expression Language modules.
Data Access/Integration: Includes JDBC, ORM, Transactions, and OXM modules.
Web: Includes Web, Web MVC, WebFlux, and WebSockets modules.
AOP (Aspect-Oriented Programming): Provides support for cross-cutting concerns like logging and security.
Instrumentation: Supports class instrumentation and classloader implementations.
Test: Provides support for testing Spring components with tools like JUnit and Mockito.

q: What are the differences between Spring and Spring Boot?
a:
Spring: A comprehensive framework requiring manual configuration of beans, dependencies, and XML files.
Spring Boot: An opinionated framework built on top of Spring that simplifies development by providing auto-configuration, embedded servers, and default settings.
Configuration: Spring requires explicit configuration, while Spring Boot uses convention over configuration.
Development Speed: Spring Boot accelerates development with starters and minimal setup.
Deployment: Spring Boot includes embedded servers (e.g., Tomcat), whereas Spring requires external server setup.

q: What are Transaction Isolation and propagation?
a:
Transaction Isolation: Defines how transactions interact with each other in terms of visibility and locking.
READ_UNCOMMITTED: Allows dirty reads.
READ_COMMITTED: Prevents dirty reads but allows non-repeatable reads.
REPEATABLE_READ: Prevents dirty reads and non-repeatable reads but allows phantom reads.
SERIALIZABLE: Prevents all types of anomalies but has the highest overhead.

Transaction Propagation: Defines how transactions relate to each other when methods call one another.
REQUIRED: Supports an existing transaction or creates a new one.
REQUIRES_NEW: Always creates a new transaction, suspending any existing one.
SUPPORTS: Executes within a transaction if one exists; otherwise, executes non-transactionally.
NOT_SUPPORTED: Executes non-transactionally, suspending any existing transaction.
MANDATORY: Requires an existing transaction; throws an exception if none exists.
NEVER: Executes non-transactionally; throws an exception if a transaction exists.

Protocol vs Architecture:
SOAP: A protocol with strict standards (XML-based).
REST: An architectural style that uses standard HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE).

Data Format:
SOAP: Uses XML exclusively.
REST: Supports multiple formats (JSON, XML, plain text).

Performance:
SOAP: Heavier due to XML parsing and strict standards.
REST: Lightweight and faster due to JSON usage.

Security:
SOAP: Built-in WS-Security for enterprise-level security.
REST: Relies on HTTPS and OAuth for security.

Use Cases:
SOAP: Suitable for enterprise-level applications requiring strict contracts.
REST: Ideal for lightweight, scalable web services and mobile applications.
